one_line_takeaway: A position paper that reframes LLM-agent security around classic Saltzer-Schroeder principles and demonstrates, via a proof-of-concept 'AgentSandbox' built on persistent/ephemeral agent separation, that principled layering cuts average prompt-injection ASR from ~58.8% to 4.34% on AgentDojo while preserving benign utility (82.0% vs 83.8% undefended).
key_finding: AgentSandbox achieves the best overall utility-security trade-off: it reduces average ASR to 4.34% while keeping average benign utility at 82.00%, comparable to the 83.81% benign utility of the No-Defense baseline (which itself suffers an average ASR the paper reports as 58.84%). It attains the lowest ASR in every one of the four suites (Banking 5.56%, Slack 3.81%, Travel 7.14%, Workspace 0.83%). Competing defenses each fail on one axis: Delimiting and Repeat Prompt keep high benign utility (75.75% / 85.75%) but leave average ASR at 27.97% / 27.33%; PI Detector drives ASR low (6.79% avg) but cripples attack utility (often below 20%) by halting the agent on any suspected injection; Tool Filter is more balanced (avg ASR 8.90%, avg attack utility 60.10%) but costs benign utility (drops to 71.24%) because it excludes whole tool categories.
core_contribution: A position paper arguing that the classic, five-decade-old information-security design principles (Saltzer & Schroeder's least privilege, complete mediation, psychological acceptability, plus the later defense-in-depth) should be explicitly and conscientiously applied when deploying LLM agents at scale, rather than the current ad hoc, low-level defenses. As a proof of concept the authors propose AgentSandbox, a conceptual framework that bakes these four principles into an agent's life-cycle. Its central design idea (which the paper claims is what distinguishes it from prior agent-security architectures) is separating a Persistent Agent (PA) that holds the long-term user profile/PII from short-lived, isolated Ephemeral Agents (EAs) that are spawned per task and discarded on completion. AgentSandbox has five components: PA, Data Minimizer (DM, context-aware access control that forwards only task-essential data to an EA), EA, I/O Firewall (static, rule-based schema/policy enforcement on every external message), and Response Filter (RF, sanitizes EA output before it returns to the PA). A 'reward modeling policy engine' (inspired by prompt optimization / DSPy, refs [28,41]) treats data-sharing policies as optimizable prompts and iteratively tunes them from observed task outcomes to serve psychological acceptability by automating policy configuration.
threat_model: The user's personal LLM agent and the user's direct input queries are assumed intrinsically benign; the defender has full control over the personal agent's design, prompts, policies, modules, and interaction protocols. The adversary controls or influences the EXTERNAL agents/tools the personal agent interacts with (e.g., a compromised flight-search or hotel-booking service returns data laced with malicious commands). Adversary goals: compromise user privacy (e.g., exfiltrate credit card number or SSN via a messaging tool) or induce malicious actions (e.g., transfer funds to an attacker-controlled account via a payment tool). Concrete attack classes the framework claims to stop: PII Extraction (blocked by least privilege / DM at Stage 6), Indirect Prompt Injection via an injected malicious 'note'/'Important message' field (blocked by complete mediation / I/O Firewall schema check at Stage 5), Memory Poisoning (contained by EA isolation + policy engine at Stage 3), and Mixed Attacks (contained by defense-in-depth isolation, at latest by Stage 2).
limitations: Self-described position paper with only a 'preliminary' evaluation of a 'conceptual' framework, so several components are architectural claims rather than fully implemented/ablated systems. The defender model assumes the personal agent and direct user queries are intrinsically benign, so it does not cover a compromised or malicious user, a compromised PA, or insider threats. Evaluation is confined to a single benchmark (AgentDojo, 97 tasks), a single attack type ('Important message'), and primarily one model (gpt-4o); generalization to other benchmarks/attacks is untested in the main results. The paper explicitly states no current security measure (including its own layers) offers formal guarantees, and the reward-modeling policy engine introduces credit-assignment and prompt-space-exploration challenges it acknowledges but does not resolve. No ablation isolating the contribution of each of the five components is presented in the extracted sections. The reported average ASR figures for the No-Defense baseline (58.84%) do not equal a simple mean of the per-suite values in Table 1 (~44.4%), suggesting a weighted/instance-level aggregation the paper does not fully specify.

datasets_used: AgentDojo benchmark (Debenedetti et al. [19]), 97 realistic tasks across four suites: Banking, Slack, Travel, Workspace, using the 'Important message' prompt-injection attack
quantitative_results: AgentSandbox average ASR: 4.34% (lowest of all defenses)
AgentSandbox average benign utility: 82.00% (vs 83.81% for No Defense)
No Defense average ASR: 58.84% (paper's stated figure)
AgentSandbox per-suite ASR: Banking 5.56%, Slack 3.81%, Travel 7.14%, Workspace 0.83%
No Defense per-suite ASR (Table 1): Banking 49.31%, Slack 74.29%, Travel 27.14%, Workspace 26.67%
AgentSandbox per-suite benign (No-Attack) Utility: Banking 87.50%, Slack 90.48%, Travel 80.00%, Workspace 70.00%
Delimiting avg benign utility 75.75%, avg ASR 27.97%; Repeat Prompt avg benign utility 85.75%, avg ASR 27.33%
PI Detector average ASR 6.79% but attack utility often below 20%
Tool Filter average attack utility 60.10%, average ASR 8.90%, benign utility drops to 71.24%
Cited external stat: advanced LLMs fail prompt-injection defenses ~85% of the time (ref [74]) -- this is background from cited work, not this paper's own measurement
baselines_compared: PI Detector [44] (fine-tuned DeBERTa prompt-injection classifier that halts the agent on detection)
Delimiting [25] (spotlighting; wrap user query in delimiters)
Repeat Prompt [43] (sandwich defense; repeat user query after each tool call)
